changeSet.author: spring6-migration
changeSet.comment: Rename hibernate_sequences value column to match Hibernate 6 default TABLE generator expectations

changeSet.author: spring6-migration

preConditions.onFailMessage: hibernate_sequences not on the Hibernate 6 layout yet; nothing to reseed
preConditions.onErrorMessage: hibernate_sequences not present / not MySQL; nothing to reseed

changeSet.comment:
RSDEV-444 (B1): re-seed the Hibernate TABLE-generator counters after the Hibernate 5 to 6 upgrade.
Under Hibernate 5 (new_generator_mappings=false) the entities annotated @GeneratedValue(strategy=TABLE) used the legacy MultipleHiLoPerTableGenerator, whose stored value in hibernate_sequences is a hi/lo "high" marker, not the next id. Hibernate 6's enhanced TableGenerator reads next_val much more literally, so the small legacy values would hand out ids at or below ids already in use, colliding on the primary key across roughly 40 tables.
This changeset loops over every existing hibernate_sequences row and, for any row whose name is a real base table with an "id" column, sets next_val above that table's MAX(id). Driving off the existing rows makes it complete by construction: a TABLE-strategy table only has data if it has a row here, so no entity can be missed (including MappedSuperclass subclasses such as Sample/SubSample/ Container and StoichiometryInventoryLink, whose ids would be easy to omit from a hand-written list).
The two TABLE_PER_CLASS hierarchies share one sequence across sibling tables and are keyed by the abstract entity name (not a table), so they are handled explicitly: - AbstractUserOrGroupImpl -> shared by User + rsGroup - Communication -> shared by Notification, MessageOrRequest, GroupMessageOrRequest, CreateGroupMessageOrRequest, ShareRecordMessageOrRequest These two carry an explicit @TableGenerator(allocationSize=1) in rspace-core-model.
Finally a "default" row is seeded above the global MAX(id). This row is the one Hibernate 6 actually allocates from for every bare @GeneratedValue(strategy=TABLE) entity (verified on a populated DB, 2026-06-06): prefer_entity_table_as_segment_value is a TableGenerator-local config parameter, not a global hibernate.* setting, so bare TABLE entities consolidate onto the shared "default" segment. The per-entity re-seeds above are belt-and-braces for the legacy rows (only the two explicit @TableGenerator hierarchies still read theirs); collision safety rests on this row.
Margin: next_val = MAX(id) + 51 clears a full default allocation block (allocationSize 50) plus one, which is safe for the pooled, pooled-lo and no-op optimizers alike. It is idempotent: re-running never lowers an existing value, and empty tables (MAX 0) are left for Hibernate to create lazily.
VERIFIED on populated DBs 2026-06-06 and 2026-06-09 (see investigations/RSDEV-444-spring6-review.md, B1/B1a): changeset applied, all segments reseeded above MAX(id), live inserts allocated from the "default" segment above the global max with no collision and no unexpected new rows.

changeSet.author: spring6-migration
changeSet.comment: Add stable UUID natural key for AppConfigElementSet so equals/hashCode are safe before and after persist. Column is added nullable and non-unique first, then populated, then constrained: adding it as NOT NULL UNIQUE up front fails on a populated table because every existing row is back-filled with the empty-string default, colliding on the unique index before the UPDATE can run.

changeSet.author: spring6-migration
changeSet.comment: Convert ExternalWorkFlowData enum columns from ordinal (integer-as-string) to enum name strings for Hibernate 6 compatibility

changeSet.author: spring6-migration

preConditions.onFailMessage: BATCH_* tables absent or already on the Spring Batch 5 layout; nothing to migrate
preConditions.onErrorMessage: Not MySQL/MariaDB or BATCH_* tables absent; nothing to migrate

changeSet.comment:
RSDEV-444 (B2): migrate the Spring Batch metadata tables from the Batch 3 layout to Batch 5 on existing deployments.
The fresh-install baseline (initial-seed-changeLog.xml) only creates these tables when BATCH_JOB_EXECUTION is ABSENT, so an upgraded database keeps the Batch 3 schema and the async export JobRepository fails at runtime against the changed columns (most notably the redesigned BATCH_JOB_EXECUTION_PARAMS). This changeset drops and recreates the BATCH_* tables using the Batch 5 jar's own scripts, so the layout always matches the Batch version on the classpath.
Guarded to fire ONLY on an un-migrated existing DB (BATCH_JOB_EXECUTION present AND the old TYPE_CD column present): a no-op on fresh installs (the baseline already built the Batch 5 schema, so TYPE_CD is absent) and idempotent once migrated (TYPE_CD gone).
TRADE-OFF: drop+recreate discards historical Spring Batch job-execution rows. These back ONLY the REST API v1 export (status polled via /api/v1/jobs/{id}); the interactive UI export uses a separate mechanism and the archive files themselves are downloaded by filename off the filestore, independent of these tables. So no archive becomes undownloadable; only the jobId-to-result lookup for an API job whose result a client had not yet fetched is lost (remedy: re-export). There is no FK into BATCH_*, so the drop is structurally safe. Chosen over a fragile hand-written ALTER across the large 3-to-5 version jump.
ID CONTINUITY: Spring Batch allocates ids from the BATCH_*_SEQ incrementer tables (next id = stored ID + 1). A plain recreate would reset them to 0 and eventually re-issue pre-upgrade jobIds. To avoid that, the current MAX ids are captured into a temporary holding table BEFORE the drop, then the recreated BATCH_*_SEQ rows are seeded to those maxima so new ids continue monotonically.
REQUIRES: the Liquibase user must be able to DROP/CREATE these tables (the app schema user has ALL privileges in the standard deployment).
